system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses (E1-E5) and 14 bipolar constructs. Rate each response on each construct on a 1-7 scale: 1 = strongly the LEFT pole, 7 = strongly the RIGHT pole, 4 = neutral or mixed.

Responses:
**E1:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy.** The decision turns on a specific asymmetry: in consumer health, trust failures are existential, capability shortfalls are merely competitive. **Why B over the alternatives:**

The local-only option (A) sounds principled but ships a structurally weaker coach. A 60-70% capability ceiling in *health guidance* isn't a minor compromise — it directly affects whether the product gives users useful or mediocre advice on conditions...

**E2:** Given the sensitive nature of health data and the high expectations of a 2M-user consumer app, **Option B — encrypted cloud with no training** — is the superior choice. It uniquely delivers full AI capability without compromising privacy, sidestepping the regulatory quagmire and trust erosion that plague data-hungry alternatives. Health coaching demands the best possible model; a local-only approach (Option A) sacrifices too much quality at a critical moment when users are judging the feature’s utility.

**E3:** I strongly recommend **Option B: Encrypted cloud with no training**. For a direct-to-consumer health app with two million active users, trust is not just a brand pillar; it is the entire foundation of the enterprise. When dealing with highly sensitive personal health data, the margin for error regarding data privacy is zero.

**E4:** I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing with no training on user health data** as the primary policy. For a direct-to-consumer health app, the core asset is not only model quality; it is user trust. Health data is among the most sensitive categories of personal information, and an AI coaching feature will likely handle behavioral, biometric, medication, reproductive, mental health, and chronic-condition signals.

**E5:** **Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted cloud with no training.**

For a consumer health app, trust is the product. Users do not compartmentalize “health data” from “identity”; a breach or even the perception of data exploitation destroys adoption faster than any feature attracts it. Option B is the only architecture that delivers full AI capability without creating a retained data footprint that must be defended forever.

Constructs (id : left pole ↔ right pole):
  C_M1_1: 'Grounds recommendation in specific health data categories (E4, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Argues from general capability-versus-privacy tradeoff (E2)'
  C_M1_2: 'Frames trust as foundational/existential to product (E3, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames choice as regulatory and quality optimization (E2)'
  C_M1_3: 'Uses vivid, absolutist rhetoric about trust (E3, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Enumerates concrete sensitive data types pragmatically (E4)'
  C_M2_1: 'Strategic trust-and-capability rhetoric'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Concrete health-data risk inventory'
  C_M2_2: 'Trust-first privacy absolutism'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Explicit alternative-by-alternative trade-off analysis'
  C_M2_3: 'Explicit local-only capability critique'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Pure privacy-and-trust rationale'
  C_M3_1: 'Focuses purely on data sensitivity and user trust'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Balances privacy needs with AI model capability constraints'
  C_M3_2: 'Rejects local-only alternatives due to inferior AI performance'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames choice entirely around avoiding retained data footprints'
  C_M4_1: 'Critiques local option's AI quality'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Omits local option critique'
  C_M4_2: 'Omits regulatory concerns'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Mentions regulatory avoidance'
  C_M4_3: 'Acknowledges model capability'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Focuses exclusively on trust'
  C_M5_1: 'Evaluates capability limitations of local processing'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Focuses solely on data privacy breach risks'
  C_M5_2: 'Acknowledges model quality as a competing priority'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Treats trust as the exclusive imperative'
  C_M5_3: 'Provides specific technical or categorical details'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Relies on abstract foundational principles'

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict). Write exactly 70 lines, no commentary, no markdown fences, no headers. One line per (construct, element) pair, in this exact format:
<construct_id>,<element>,<rating>

Example (showing the format only - your numbers will differ):
C_M1_1,E1,1
C_M1_1,E2,2
C_M1_1,E3,3
C_M1_2,E1,4
...

Constraints:
- rating is an integer from 1 to 7
- iterate all 14 constructs in the order listed, and within each construct all 5 elements in order (E1-E5)
- produce 70 lines total
- output ONLY the lines, nothing before or after

message.reasoning:
We are given 5 responses and 14 constructs. Each construct is a bipolar scale from 1 to 7. We need to rate each element on each construct. The constructs are labeled C_M1_1 through C_M5_3. The description of each construct gives a left pole (1) and right pole (7). We must interpret each response and assign a rating based on how much it aligns with the left or right pole.

Let's list constructs with their left and right poles:

C_M1_1: left: "Grounds recommendation in specific health data categories (E4, E5)" ; right: "Argues from general capability-versus-privacy tradeoff (E2)". So a rating of 1 means the response grounds in specific health data categories like E4 and E5 do. A rating of 7 means argues from general capability-versus-privacy tradeoff like E2. Note: the parenthetical mentions which responses exemplify that pole. We'll use that as guide.

C_M1_2: left: "Frames trust as foundational/existential to product (E3, E5)" ; right: "Frames choice as regulatory and quality optimization (E2)". 

C_M1_3: left: "Uses vivid, absolutist rhetoric about trust (E3, E5)" ; right: "Enumerates concrete sensitive data types pragmatically (E4)". 

C_M2_1: left: "Strategic trust-and-capability rhetoric" ; right: "Concrete health-data risk inventory". Note: no parenthetical examples. So we need to interpret.

C_M2_2: left: "Trust-first privacy absolutism" ; right: "Explicit alternative-by-alternative trade-off analysis".

C_M2_3: left: "Explicit local-only capability critique" ; right: "Pure privacy-and-trust rationale".

C_M3_1: left: "Focuses purely on data sensitivity and user trust" ; right: "Balances privacy needs with AI model capability constraints".

C_M3_2: left: "Rejects local-only alternatives due to inferior AI performance" ; right: "Frames choice entirely around avoiding retained data footprints".

C_M4_1: left: "Critiques local option's AI quality" ; right: "Omits local option critique". So left pole means the response criticizes local option's AI quality; right pole means it does not mention local option critique.

C_M4_2: left: "Omits regulatory concerns" ; right: "Mentions regulatory avoidance". So left pole means no mention of regulatory; right pole means mentions regulatory avoidance.

C_M4_3: left: "Acknowledges model capability" ; right: "Focuses exclusively on trust". So left means acknowledges model capability as a factor; right means only focuses on trust.

C_M5_1: left: "Evaluates capability limitations of local processing" ; right: "Focuses solely on data privacy breach risks".

C_M5_2: left: "Acknowledges model quality as a competing priority" ; right: "Treats trust as the exclusive imperative".

C_M5_3: left: "Provides specific technical or categorical details" ; right: "Relies on abstract foundational principles".

Now we need to read each response and assign a rating for each construct.

Let's summarize each response:

E1: "Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training. For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, Option B is the right primary policy. The decision turns on a specific asymmetry: in consumer health, trust failures are existential, capability shortfalls are merely competitive. Why B over the alternatives: The local-only option (A) sounds principled but ships a structurally weaker coach. A 60-70% capability ceiling in health guidance isn't a minor compromise — it directly affects whether the product gives users useful or mediocre advice on conditions..." So E1 critiques local option's capability (60-70% ceiling), frames trust as existential but also acknowledges capability tradeoff. It mentions "trust failures are existential, capability shortfalls are merely competitive" - so trust is foundational but capability is also considered. It does not enumerate specific health data categories; it talks in general about health guidance. It uses some vivid language ("existential", "structurally weaker"). It does not mention regulatory concerns explicitly. It provides a critique of local option's AI quality.

E2: "Given the sensitive nature of health data and the high expectations of a 2M-user consumer app, Option B — encrypted cloud with no training — is the superior choice. It uniquely delivers full AI capability without compromising privacy, sidestepping the regulatory quagmire and trust erosion that plague data-hungry alternatives. Health coaching demands the best possible model; a local-only approach (Option A) sacrifices too much quality at a critical moment when users are judging the feature’s utility." So E2 mentions "sensitive nature of health data" but not specific categories. It frames choice as involving regulatory quagmire and trust erosion. It argues from capability-versus-privacy tradeoff: "delivers full AI capability without compromising privacy". It critiques local option ("sacrifices too much quality"). It mentions regulatory avoidance ("sidestepping the regulatory quagmire"). It acknowledges model quality as a priority competing with privacy? It says "health coaching demands the best possible model", so model quality is important. It uses less absolutist rhetoric but still mentions "trust erosion".

E3: "I strongly recommend Option B: Encrypted cloud with no training. For a direct-to-consumer health app with two million active users, trust is not just a brand pillar; it is the entire foundation of the enterprise. When dealing with highly sensitive personal health data, the margin for error regarding data privacy is zero." So E3 focuses heavily on trust, uses absolutist rhetoric ("entire foundation", "margin for error is zero"). Does not critique local option's capability explicitly; does not mention regulatory concerns; does not enumerate data categories; frames trust as existential.

E4: "I recommend Option B: encrypted cloud processing with no training on user health data as the primary policy. For a direct-to-consumer health app, the core asset is not only model quality; it is user trust. Health data is among the most sensitive categories of personal information, and an AI coaching feature will likely handle behavioral, biometric, medication, reproductive, mental health, and chronic-condition signals." So E4 enumerates specific sensitive data types (behavioral, biometric, etc.). It frames trust as core asset but also acknowledges model quality ("not only model quality"). It does not critique local option's capability explicitly; it does not mention regulatory concerns; it uses pragmatic enumeration of data types rather than absolutist rhetoric.

E5: "Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted cloud with no training. For a consumer health app, trust is the product. Users do not compartmentalize “health data” from “identity”; a breach or even the perception of data exploitation destroys adoption faster than any feature attracts it. Option B is the only architecture that delivers full AI capability without creating a retained data footprint that must be defended forever." So E5 frames trust as the product, uses vivid language ("trust is the product", "destroys adoption"). It mentions "full AI capability" so acknowledges capability. It does not critique local option's AI quality directly (but implies by saying "only architecture that delivers full AI capability"). It does not enumerate specific data categories but mentions "health data" and "identity". It focuses on avoiding retained data footprint. It does not mention regulatory concerns. It uses absolutist rhetoric ("destroys adoption faster than any feature attracts it").

messages.content: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.

You are presented with 5 anonymous responses (E1-E5) and 14 bipolar constructs. Rate each response on each construct on a 1-7 scale: 1 = strongly the LEFT pole, 7 = strongly the RIGHT pole, 4 = neutral or mixed.

Responses:
**E1:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy.** The decision turns on a specific asymmetry: in consumer health, trust failures are existential, capability shortfalls are merely competitive. **Why B over the alternatives:**

The local-only option (A) sounds principled but ships a structurally weaker coach. A 60-70% capability ceiling in *health guidance* isn't a minor compromise — it directly affects whether the product gives users useful or mediocre advice on conditions...

**E2:** Given the sensitive nature of health data and the high expectations of a 2M-user consumer app, **Option B — encrypted cloud with no training** — is the superior choice. It uniquely delivers full AI capability without compromising privacy, sidestepping the regulatory quagmire and trust erosion that plague data-hungry alternatives. Health coaching demands the best possible model; a local-only approach (Option A) sacrifices too much quality at a critical moment when users are judging the feature’s utility.

**E3:** I strongly recommend **Option B: Encrypted cloud with no training**. For a direct-to-consumer health app with two million active users, trust is not just a brand pillar; it is the entire foundation of the enterprise. When dealing with highly sensitive personal health data, the margin for error regarding data privacy is zero.

**E4:** I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing with no training on user health data** as the primary policy. For a direct-to-consumer health app, the core asset is not only model quality; it is user trust. Health data is among the most sensitive categories of personal information, and an AI coaching feature will likely handle behavioral, biometric, medication, reproductive, mental health, and chronic-condition signals.

**E5:** **Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted cloud with no training.**

For a consumer health app, trust is the product. Users do not compartmentalize “health data” from “identity”; a breach or even the perception of data exploitation destroys adoption faster than any feature attracts it. Option B is the only architecture that delivers full AI capability without creating a retained data footprint that must be defended forever.

Constructs (id : left pole ↔ right pole):
  C_M1_1: 'Grounds recommendation in specific health data categories (E4, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Argues from general capability-versus-privacy tradeoff (E2)'
  C_M1_2: 'Frames trust as foundational/existential to product (E3, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames choice as regulatory and quality optimization (E2)'
  C_M1_3: 'Uses vivid, absolutist rhetoric about trust (E3, E5)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Enumerates concrete sensitive data types pragmatically (E4)'
  C_M2_1: 'Strategic trust-and-capability rhetoric'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Concrete health-data risk inventory'
  C_M2_2: 'Trust-first privacy absolutism'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Explicit alternative-by-alternative trade-off analysis'
  C_M2_3: 'Explicit local-only capability critique'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Pure privacy-and-trust rationale'
  C_M3_1: 'Focuses purely on data sensitivity and user trust'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Balances privacy needs with AI model capability constraints'
  C_M3_2: 'Rejects local-only alternatives due to inferior AI performance'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Frames choice entirely around avoiding retained data footprints'
  C_M4_1: 'Critiques local option's AI quality'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Omits local option critique'
  C_M4_2: 'Omits regulatory concerns'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Mentions regulatory avoidance'
  C_M4_3: 'Acknowledges model capability'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Focuses exclusively on trust'
  C_M5_1: 'Evaluates capability limitations of local processing'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Focuses solely on data privacy breach risks'
  C_M5_2: 'Acknowledges model quality as a competing priority'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Treats trust as the exclusive imperative'
  C_M5_3: 'Provides specific technical or categorical details'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Relies on abstract foundational principles'

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict). Write exactly 70 lines, no commentary, no markdown fences, no headers. One line per (construct, element) pair, in this exact format:
<construct_id>,<element>,<rating>

Example (showing the format only - your numbers will differ):
C_M1_1,E1,1
C_M1_1,E2,2
C_M1_1,E3,3
C_M1_2,E1,4
...

Constraints:
- rating is an integer from 1 to 7
- iterate all 14 constructs in the order listed, and within each construct all 5 elements in order (E1-E5)
- produce 70 lines total
- output ONLY the lines, nothing before or after
